entry.title: Client Certificate Signing for Integration Suite - An iFlow That Does the Heavy Lifting
entry.content:
Public Certificate Authorities are phasing out the Client Authentication EKU from publicly trusted SSL/TLS certificates. For inbound scenarios that use Client Certificate Authentication to connect to Cloud Integration or API Management, that EKU is mandatory; certificates without it get rejected at runtime, typically surfacing as an HTTP 401 Unauthorized error on the sender side.

For the full background on this change and the available remediation options, see the SAP KBA 3711618 . for a step-by-step tutorial on the manual way to do that, follow this blog post Integration Suite - Client Authentication EKU deprecation. This blog offers an easy, user-friendly way to do it, all by simply deploying an iFlow.

We have two versions on offer:

  • iFlow v1 — full self-service: if you'd rather avoid the technical details of generating a private key and CSR yourself, this version handles all of that for you, end to end.
  • iFlow v2 — sign-only: if you'd prefer not to expose your private key to the CPI or iFlow at all, you van generate your private key and CSR separately (e.g., via OpenSSL or your own..), and use this version purely to get your certificate signed by the SAP Cloud Root CA.

Both iFlows need a Destination service instance and service key to call the signing API. How you get there depends on your environment:

  • Cloud Foundry subaccount: subscribe to the Destination service and create a service key directly in that subaccount.
  • NEO subaccount: you'll need a Cloud Foundry subaccount as well, since the Destination service used here runs on Cloud Foundry. Creating one alongside your existing NEO subaccount can be done free of charge, with no additional costs involved.

For the step-by-step subscription and service-key creation process, follow the first part of this blog: 
👉SAP Integration Suite - Client Authentication EKU deprecation

Once you've subscribed and created the service key, you'll be left with a service key JSON that looks like this:

 

 

we'll use the clientid, clientsecret, url (token endpoint), and uri (Destination service API endpoint) values from this JSON when configuring the iFlow in the next section.

 

 

 

I. Full Self-Service – Client Certificate Signing via BTP Destination Service v1
If you'd rather not go through the technical the Client Certificate Signing via BTP Destination Service v1 iFlow takes care of all of it for you. The iflow works on both Cloud Foundry and NEO tenants. Under the hood, it handles:

  • Generating a private key
  • Generating a CSR
  • Converting the CSR to Base64
  • Fetching an OAuth2 access token
  • Making the CSR signing call to the Destination service
  • Retrieving the signed certificate
  • Converting the response back from Base64
  • Attaching the private key and signed certificate.

All you have to do is:
Step 1 — Import the iFlow into your CPI tenant

 

Download the iflow v1 ZIP file, and then in your cloud Integration tenant go to Design and then choose your desired package or create one, and click on Edit. And then click on Add, then Integration Flow:

 

 

 

You will get a pop up, chose the Upload Option, and then from your downloads choose the Iflow ZIP file that you just downloaded:

And finally click on Add and Open in Editor:

Step 2 — Configure the iflow
Next, navigate to configure. Fill in the following receiver parameters:

  • The authentication server, which is the URL from the service key:

  • In the receiver field, open the dropbox, and choose the destination server, and fill it with the URI from the service key:

 

  • Next Navigate to more, and configure the rest of the parameters (desired certificate name and CN, client ID and Secret):

Step 3 — Save the configurations & deploy the iflow
Save the configuration, and deploy the iflow:

Finally, go to Monitor Message Processing and filter by the iFlow name. The completed message has two files attached: private_key.pem (the private key generated for your certificate) and CertNameExample.pem (the signed certificate from the SAP Cloud Root CA). Download both and store them.

II. Sign only – Client Certificate Signing via BTP Destination Service v2
If you’d rather not expose your private key to Cloud Integration and the iFlow, you can generate it yourself, either via OpenSSL or directly from your backend system’s STRUST. In that case, the Client Certificate Signing via BTP Destination Service v2 iFlow is your safe choice. Given your CSR, this iFlow takes care of:

  • Converting the CSR to Base64
  • Fetching an OAuth2 access token
  • Making the CSR signing call to the Destination service
  • Retrieving the signed certificate
  • Converting the response back from Base64
  • Attaching the signed certificate.
All you have to do is:
Step 1 — Import the iFlow into your CPI tenant
 
 
 
Download the iflow v2 ZIP file, and then in your cloud Integration tenant go to Design and then choose your desired package or create one, and click on Edit. And then click on Add, then Integration Flow:
 
 
 
 
 
 
You will get a pop up, chose the Upload Option, and then from your downloads choose the Iflow ZIP file that you just downloaded: 
 
And finally click on Add and Open in Editor:
 
Step 2 — Configure the iflow

Next, navigate to configure. Fill in the following receiver parameters:

  • The authentication server, which is the URL from the service key:

 
  • In the receiver field, open the dropbox, and choose the destination server, and fill it with the URI from the service key:

 
Next Navigate to More, and configure the rest of the parameters (desired certificate name and CN, client ID and Secret, and CSR to sign):
 
Step 3 — Save the configurations & deploy the iflow
Next , Save the configuration, and deploy the iflow:
 
Finally, go to Monitor Message Processing and filter by the iFlow name. The completed message has a file attached: CertNameExample.pem (the signed certificate from the SAP Cloud Root CA). Download and store it.
 
Notes: 
  • The private key and the signed certificate are both returned in PEM format.
  • The signed certificate you receive is actually a full chain: the leaf (your client certificate), one or more intermediates (depending on your tenant’s region), and the root, all concatenated into a single file. Open it in a text editor and copy each block delimited by -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- and -----END CERTIFICATE----- into its own file: the first block is the leaf certificate, the next one(s) are the intermediate(s), and the last is the SAP Cloud Root CA. Save each one in whatever format you prefer (.cer works well if you just want to double-click and inspect it).
  • Ensure all Destination service configuration values are entered correctly. Even a single extra character (such as a space or line break) can cause errors.

entry.title: An XI (ABAP Proxy) Sender Simulator for SAP Integration Testing
entry.content:
Introduction

Have you ever wanted to test one inbound ABAP Proxy interface, but before sending one single message you first had to prepare a PI, PO, or SAP Cloud Integration flow?

I have run into this many times.

Sometimes I only want to answer a simple question: can this SAP system receive this XI message, and does the payload match the expected interface? But the setup ends up bigger than the test itself. You may need a sender flow, a receiver channel, routing, credentials, certificates, sample payloads, and sometimes help from another team.

For daily development, SAP AIF testing, and incident analysis, this is slow.

So I built a small local tool: an XI (ABAP Proxy) Sender Simulator.

It sends SAP XI 3.0 multipart messages directly to the ABAP XI Engine endpoint. 

The goal is not to replace middleware. The goal is to make local testing and troubleshooting faster.

Where the tool sits

In a normal landscape, a client system reaches the ABAP inbound proxy through CPI / PI / PO. The simulator skips that whole sender flow and talks straight to the XI engine on the receiver:

This is not a replacement for the real middleware path. It is a focused shortcut for the cases where you only need to test the receiver side.

Why I built it

This idea is not new for me. I wanted to build this kind of tool many years ago.

The pain was already clear. I often needed a quick way to send one XI message to one SAP system, especially when working on SAP AIF and ABAP Proxy interfaces. I knew a small sender simulator would save time.

But I never had enough time to build it properly.

AI coding assistants change that. AI does not replace SAP integration knowledge. I still need to understand XI 3.0, ABAP Proxy, AIF, payload namespaces, QoS, authentication, and how to test all of it. But AI helps a lot with implementation, refactoring, tests, UI work, and documentation.

It lets me finish ideas I had for years but could not complete before because of limited time and energy. This tool is one of them.

What the tool does

The tool gives you a local web UI for sending XI 3.0 messages.

The basic flow is simple:

  1. Enter the SAP XI Engine URL and user.
  2. Enter sender and receiver routing information.
  3. Paste or choose a WSDL.
  4. Parse the WSDL.
  5. Generate a sample payload.
  6. Edit the payload.
  7. Send the XI message.
  8. Check the SAP response and local history.

Visually, one round-trip looks like this:

The tool builds the XI 3.0 request for you:

  • XI SOAP envelope
  • message ID
  • sender and receiver party / component
  • interface namespace and name
  • QoS mode
  • multipart/related HTTP body
  • payload cid: reference

Then it sends the request to an endpoint like:

/sap/xi/engine?type=receiver&sap-client=<client>

How it works

An XI 3.0 HTTP request is a multipart/related message.

It contains two main parts:

  • Part 1: the XI SOAP envelope, with protocol and routing metadata.
  • Part 2: the business payload XML.

The SOAP envelope references the payload by cid:.  Visually:

The tool reads the WSDL and pulls out the information it needs:

  • service interface name
  • interface namespace
  • request payload element
  • payload namespace
  • synchronous or asynchronous mode

For asynchronous interfaces the tool uses ExactlyOnce. For synchronous interfaces it uses BestEffort. The tool decides this from the WSDL operation: if the operation has an output message, it treats it as synchronous.

This WSDL parsing is important. In real SAP projects, the service interface namespace and the payload message type namespace are not always the same. Mixing them up leads to errors like INTERFACE_REGISTRATION_ERROR or INTERFACE_SIGNATURE_ERROR.

If you want to dig into the wire format itself (every field in the SOAP envelope, what type=receiver does, how to read <SAP:Error> codes), I keep my own working notes here: docs/xi-protocol.md. Those are not official SAP documentation, just what I have learned from debugging.

When it is useful

This tool is useful when you want to:

  • test an inbound ABAP Proxy interface quickly
  • reproduce an SAP AIF inbound issue
  • check whether the interface namespace and name are correct
  • check whether the payload root element and namespace are correct
  • generate a first sample payload from a WSDL
  • separate transport / protocol errors from application errors
  • do a quick regression test without rebuilding a full middleware flow

The main value is a shorter feedback loop.

Try it

GitHub https://github.com/LF-LLL/xi-abap-proxy-sender-simulator

Safety and scope

This tool is not an official SAP tool.

It is not delivered, supported, or endorsed by SAP.

It is not designed for production message processing.

It should not be used as a production integration component, retry tool, or monitoring tool.

It is a local development and testing helper. The target users are developers, integration consultants, and operations / support colleagues who need to validate or reproduce integration messages more efficiently.

The tool can send authenticated HTTP requests to SAP systems, so it has some safety boundaries:

  • It listens on 127.0.0.1 by default.
  • It refuses non-localhost binding unless the user explicitly opts in.
  • It uses a per-start token to reduce accidental local or cross-site calls.
  • It only accepts send URLs whose path looks like a SAP endpoint, such as /sap/... or /XISOAPAdapter/....
  • It does not return password defaults from the local config file back to the browser.
  • It does not store passwords in history.
  • It redacts sensitive response headers.
  • It truncates large payloads and responses before storing them in local history.

Even with all of that, please treat it as a lab tool. Run it only on your own machine, or in a controlled test environment.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public WM – How to group multiple picking tasks with the same destination?
entry.content:
English Version : 

Message:

Hello everyone,

I am currently working on a SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Warehouse Management) project, and I am facing an operational requirement that many of you have probably encountered in EWM or WM environments.

Functional context:

  • Several warehouse tasks are generated (e.g., 10 tasks).

  • The source storage bins are different.

  • The destination is the same (shipping area, production staging area, etc.).

  • Today, the warehouse operator must execute each task one by one, resulting in many unnecessary trips.

Issue:

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public WM, I cannot find any functionality that would allow:

  • picking everything at once,

  • or grouping multiple picking tasks into a single movement, as it would be possible in SAP EWM Private Cloud / On-Premise (e.g., Two-Step Picking, advanced grouping, consolidation logic).

My question:

Based on your experience, what are the realistic options available in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public WM to:

  • reduce back-and-forth movements,

  • optimize multi-task picking,

  • group or orchestrate several tasks sharing the same destination,

  • or work around this limitation using standard processes (waves, RF, Pick HU, etc.)?

If you have already faced this situation in a Public Cloud context, I would greatly appreciate your insights, best practices, or recommendations.

Thank you in advance for your help.

En français :

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public WM – Regroupement de tâches de prélèvement (picking) : quelles options disponibles ?

Message :

Bonjour à tous,

Je travaille actuellement sur un projet SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Warehouse Management), et je rencontre un besoin opérationnel que plusieurs d’entre vous ont probablement déjà affronté dans des environnements EWM ou WM.

Contexte fonctionnel :

  • Plusieurs tâches de magasin sont générées (ex. 10 tâches).

  • Les emplacements sources sont différents.

  • L’emplacement de destination est identique (zone d’expédition, zone de staging pour OF, etc.).

  • Aujourd’hui, le magasinier doit exécuter les tâches une par une, ce qui entraîne de nombreux allers-retours.

Problème :

Dans SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public WM, je ne trouve pas de fonctionnalité permettant de :

  • prélever tout d’un coup,

  • ou regrouper plusieurs tâches de prélèvement en un seul mouvement, comme cela serait possible dans SAP EWM Private Cloud / On-Premise (ex. Two-Step Picking, regroupements avancés, logiques de consolidation).

Ma question :

Quelles sont, selon votre expérience, les options réellement disponibles dans SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public WM pour :

  • réduire les allers-retours,

  • optimiser le picking multi-tâches,

  • regrouper ou orchestrer plusieurs tâches ayant un même emplacement de destination,

  • ou contourner cette limitation via des processus standards (vagues, RF, Pick HU, etc.) ?

Si vous avez déjà été confrontés à ce sujet dans un contexte Public Cloud, je serais très intéressé par vos retours d’expérience, vos bonnes pratiques ou vos recommandations.

Merci d’avance pour votre aide.

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Supply Chain

entry.title: Re: Revert the changes from Custom Field and logic app
entry.content:
Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. I'm very sorry for the delay.

#GenAI-Assisted Content

I asked our internal chatbot for support, and here's the response. Please check whether the suggestions help you resolve your issue:

It seems you are encountering an error when trying to delete custom fields XREF1, XREF2, and XREF3 from the ACDOCA table using the Custom Field and Logic app. This issue might be related to the limitations or dependencies associated with custom fields in SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

Possible Causes and Solutions

  1. Dependencies: If the custom fields are being used in other applications or reports, you may not be able to delete them directly. You need to ensure that these fields are not referenced elsewhere.

  2. Business Context Capacity: If the business context capacity is exhausted, you might face issues when trying to modify or delete fields. You can check the capacity usage and try to delete unused fields to free up space.

  3. Production Environment Restrictions: Changes to custom fields might not be allowed directly in the production environment. 2543840 - Editing is not Allowed in Extensibility Apps You should make these changes in a test environment and then transport them to production.

  4. Error Messages: If you are receiving specific error messages, they might provide more insight into the issue. For example, if the error is related to ‘Business context capacity is exhausted’, 2824028 - Error ‘Field cannot be created. Business context capacity is exhausted’ when creating a new custom field it indicates a limitation in the number of fields or characters that can be added.

Steps to Resolve

  • Check Dependencies: Use the Custom CDS Views app to check if the fields are used in any CDS views or analytical queries. Remove these dependencies before attempting to delete the fields.

  • Test Environment: Make changes in a test environment and transport them to production.

  • Review Error Messages: Pay attention to the specific error messages you receive, as they can guide you to the exact issue.

If these steps do not resolve the issue, you may need to consult with your SAP administrator or SAP support for further assistance.

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Re: Searching for API with Scenario 6GU- Service Contract...
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. 

I asked our internal chatbot for support, and here's the response. Please check whether the suggestions help you resolve your issue:

Service Contract Data Transfer with Variant Configuration

Transfer from External System to SAP S/4HANA

To transfer service contract data, including variant configuration data, from an external system into SAP S/4HANA, you can use the Service Contract SOAP API. This API allows for the creation and update of service contracts asynchronously. However, it does not directly handle variant configuration data.

For variant configuration, you might need to use additional integration methods or APIs that support variant configuration data, such as SAP Variant Configuration and Pricing. This integration can be complex and may require custom development or middleware solutions to map and transfer the variant configuration data alongside the service contract data.

Transfer from SAP S/4HANA to a Third-Party System

To transfer service contract data, including variant configuration data, from SAP S/4HANA to a third-party system, you can use the Service Contract - Send Confirmation outbound service. This service sends confirmation and error messages related to service contract creation or updates.

For variant configuration data, similar to the inbound process, you may need to use additional APIs or integration solutions that can handle the variant configuration data. The SAP Variant Configuration and Pricing  integration can be used to ensure that variant configuration data is included in the transfer. For more information, see SAP CPQ Integration with SAP S/4 HANA Cloud, Public Edition.

Summary

  • Inbound to SAP S/4HANA: Use the Service Contract SOAP API  for service contract data and consider additional integration for variant configuration. For more information, see Service Contract - Create, Update (Asynchronous).
  • Outbound from SAP S/4HANA: Use the Service Contract - Send Confirmation service and additional integration for variant configuration.

For both scenarios, leveraging SAP’s integration capabilities and middleware solutions like SAP Cloud Integration can facilitate the transfer of complex data structures such as variant configurations.

I hope this helps you solve your issue.

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Re: 创建了项目参数文件：有收入的项目后，对项目进行报工，并进行了作业的实际成本率的计算后，在运行实际成本核算后，无法将作业的实际成本计入到项目中，希望得到您的帮助和支持？
entry.content: SAP Machine Translation Portal translation:

entry.title: Re: 创建了项目参数文件：有收入的项目后，对项目进行报工，并进行了作业的实际成本率的计算后，在运行实际成本核算后...
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. 

I asked our internal chatbot for support, and here's the response. Please check whether the suggestions help you resolve your issue:

Understanding the Issue with Actual Cost Rate Calculation

It seems that you are facing an issue where the difference between the planned cost rate and the actual cost rate of the activity type is not being included in the project cost after running the actual cost accounting. Here are some steps and considerations to help resolve this:

  1. Check Configuration Settings: Ensure that the configuration settings for the actual cost rate calculation are correctly defined. The splitting structure should be set up to allocate costs appropriately between activity types.

  2. Verify Actual Price Indicator: The Actual Price Indicator must be set to ‘005’  in the activity type master data to ensure that actual cost rates are calculated automatically based on the activity. For more information, see Overhead Accounting - Actual Cost Rate Calculation - Cost Centers.

  3. Revaluation of Cost Centers: After calculating the actual cost rates, you should revalue the receiving cost centers using the ‘Edit Actual Costing Run’ app. This step is crucial to balance sender cost centers and reflect the actual cost rates in project costs.

  4. Check for Errors: Ensure there are no errors during the splitting process  or the actual cost rate calculation. Errors can prevent the correct allocation of costs. For more information, see 3055021 - KP250 error happens in App Actual Cost Rate Calculation,

  5. Use the Correct App: Make sure you are using the ‘Actual Cost Rate Calculation - Cost Centers’ app correctly, as it calculates iterative cost rates for activity types based on actual costs and activities performed.

If these steps do not resolve the issue, it may be beneficial to consult with a configuration expert or SAP support for further assistance.

More information:'

[1] Overhead Accounting - Actual Cost Rate Calculation - Cost Centers
[2] 3055021 - KP250 error happens in App Actual Cost Rate Calculation

Kind regards,
Henrike

entry.title: SAP Ariba and SAP Fieldglass Earn 2026 TrustRadius Top Rated and Buyer's Choice Awards
entry.content:
We're proud to share that SAP Ariba and SAP Fieldglass have each earned two prestigious 2026 TrustRadius awards, recognition that means a great deal because it comes entirely from the people who matter most: our customers.

 

 SAP Ariba has been named a 2026 TrustRadius Top Rated and 2026 Buyer's Choice winner in the Procurement category, while SAP Fieldglass has received the same honors in the Vendor Management category.

 

 

What These Awards Mean

TrustRadius awards are unlike many industry accolades. The Top Rated award is not a participation award, but rather it is hard-earned and based entirely on active customer sentiment, with no analyst input and no preference for TrustRadius customers. The Buyer's Choice award goes even further, signifying that a product goes above and beyond in meeting expectations across capabilities, value, and customer relationships.

In short: these awards aren't given — they're earned, review by review, from verified users sharing their real experiences.

Recognized by the People Who Use These Products Every Day

For SAP Ariba, customers consistently highlight its ability to streamline end-to-end procurement, from sourcing and contracting to supplier management and compliance. For SAP Fieldglass, users point to its strength in managing the full contingent workforce lifecycle, from onboarding external workers to time tracking, statements of work, and payment.

Earning recognition in both the Top Rated and Buyer's Choice categories across Procurement and Vendor Management reflects SAP's ongoing commitment to delivering Spend Management solutions that drive real business value, not just on paper, but in practice, for the organizations relying on these tools every day.

Thank You to Our Customers

These awards belong to the customers who took the time to share their honest feedback on TrustRadius. Your voices shape how the market understands these products, and they inspire us to keep improving.

If you'd like to explore what customers are saying about SAP Ariba or SAP Fieldglass, visit their profiles on TrustRadius and read the verified reviews firsthand.

entry.title: CX Onboarding: Driving Successful Adoption in SAP CX: From Go-Live to Long-Term Value
entry.content:
Driving Successful Adoption in SAP CX: From Go-Live to Long-Term Value

Implementing a new cloud solution is an exciting milestone but it’s not the finish line. In fact, it’s just the beginning. The real measure of success lies in how effectively your organization adopts the solution over time and translates it into tangible business value.

In this post, we explore what “adoption” really means in the SAP Customer Experience (CX) context, why it matters, and how you can build a structured, people-centered approach that ensures long-term success.

Adoption is a Journey

One of the most important mindset shifts is understanding that adoption doesn’t end at go-live. Instead, it’s an ongoing activity that spans the entire solution lifecycle.

Adoption is about sustained usage—ensuring that users not only log into the system, but actively use it to perform their daily work, continuously embrace new features, and evolve with changing business needs. It also includes the psychological journey users go through when transitioning to a new system: learning, adapting, and ultimately trusting the solution.

When done right, adoption delivers clear benefits:

  • Faster time to value
  • Improved customer experience and satisfaction
  • Increased customer loyalty and retention

In short, adoption bridges the gap between technology and people, turning implementation into business impact.

Embedding Adoption Into Your Implementation Strategy

A common mistake is treating adoption as an afterthought. In reality, it should be embedded into your implementation approach from the very beginning.

Within the SAP Activate methodology, adoption is not a standalone activity—it’s integrated across all phases:

  • Discover phase: Start thinking about adoption before the project formally begins.
  • Explore phase: Define a clear user adoption strategy aligned with business goals.
  • Realize and Deploy: Execute enablement, communication, and engagement activities.
  • Run: Continuously improve adoption and drive innovation.

Adoption also goes hand in hand with Organizational Change Management (OCM). If you don’t prepare users for change, even the most powerful solution can fail to deliver value.

Adoption Is a Team Sport

Another key principle: adoption is not owned by a single person or department. It requires collaboration across multiple stakeholders.

Here are the core roles involved:

  • Adoption Ambassadors act as connectors—facilitating communication, gathering feedback, and ensuring alignment across teams.
  • Business Owners define the vision, goals, and expected business value.
  • IT and Project Teams enable and maintain the solution.
  • End Users bring the system to life through daily usage and feedback.

The role of the Adoption Ambassador is particularly important. They help translate business goals into user behavior, ensure consistent communication, and keep adoption on track beyond implementation. Ask yourself: has your organization appointed someone as the adoption ambassador?

Preparing for Success: Define, Measure, Communicate

Successful adoption doesn’t happen by accident—it requires a structured approach. Three pillars are critical:

1. Define Success Criteria

Start by clearly defining what success looks like for your organization. This could include:

  • Business impact KPIs
  • Improvements in customer experience
  • Increased user satisfaction
  • Achievement of project objectives

Without clear criteria, it’s impossible to measure progress or demonstrate value.

2. Measure What Matters

Adoption should be continuously monitored using a mix of quantitative and qualitative inputs:

  • System usage data
  • Business dashboards
  • Surveys and feedback from users and stakeholders
  • Indicators like awareness and engagement across business units

Measurement is not just about tracking progress—it’s about identifying gaps and opportunities for improvement.

3. Communicate Early and Often

Communication is often underestimated but plays a huge role in adoption success.

Effective communication should:

  • Clearly explain the “what, why, when, and how”
  • Start early—well before go-live
  • Provide channels for feedback and discussion
  • Highlight milestones and celebrate successes

Practical tools can include intranet pages, newsletters, collaboration platforms, FAQs, and community groups. The goal is to create transparency and build excitement.

From Adoption to Maturity

Adoption is not static—it evolves over time. This is where the concept of adoption maturity becomes valuable.

A maturity model typically progresses through stages such as:

  • Crawl: Initial system usage
  • Walk: Improved data quality and consistency
  • Run: Data-driven decision-making
  • Fly: Advanced capabilities and innovation

At each stage, the focus shifts:

  1. Active usage: Are users actually using the system?
  2. Data quality: Is the data reliable and complete?
  3. Business insights: Are you using the data to steer the business?
  4. Innovation: Are you leveraging the system to create new value?

The goal is meaningful adoption that drives better outcomes.

Enablement: The Foundation of Adoption

At its core, adoption is driven by people—and people need support.

A strong enablement strategy should:

  • Encourage: Clearly explain benefits and provide incentives
  • Provide: Allocate time and resources for learning
  • Utilize: Take advantage of SAP learning tools and resources
  • Protect: Capture and document knowledge for future use

Resources such as SAP Learning, the Help Portal, Onboarding Resource Center, and the SAP Community play a critical role in building user confidence and competence.

 

In conclusion, adoption is the difference between implementing a system and actually transforming your business.

By embedding adoption into your implementation approach, aligning stakeholders, communicating effectively, and continuously measuring and improving, you can unlock the full value of your SAP CX solutions.

For all our recommendations and best practices, please watch our dedicated 📺video series on Adoption here, and explore our 📔guide here. 

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Thank you for reading this blog. We hope you found the content insightful. Please give us a like or leave a comment; we’d love to hear from you!

You can request a one‑on‑one call with one of our onboarding advisors using this form. 

 Want to get in touch with us? Send an email to SAP_CX_Onboarding@sap.com.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Key Resources

  • Community: How to Prevent and Manage Project Risks Using SAP Best Practices
  • Community: Organizational Change Management as the Key to a Successful CX Implementation

entry.title: SSL/TLS handshake
entry.content:
I am trying to configure the connection between an SAP S/4HANA on-premise system and SAP BTP for SAP Forms Service by Adobe.

I have already completed the following steps:

  • Created the SSL Client PSE and imported the required certificates.
  • Configured OAuth 2.0.
  • Configured the RFC destination.

However, the SSL handshake still fails when I test the connection.

Has anyone encountered the same error (SSSLERR_NO_CONTEXT_YET / SSSLRC_CONN_CLOSED) when configuring SAP Forms Service by Adobe with SAP BTP?

What could be the root cause? Is this typically related to the TLS version, cipher suites, CommonCryptoLib, the SSL certificate chain, or another configuration issue?

Error message:

ERROR during SecuSSL_WriteNB() from SSL::SiRecv() == SSSLRC_CONN_CLOSED

ERROR => SecuSSL_SessionStartNB(): incomplete initial SSL/TLS handshake [ssslsecu.c 6564]

ERROR => SSL handshake with xxxxxxxxxx.authentication.eu20.hana.ondemand.com:443 failed:

SSSLRC_CONN_CLOSED (-10)

SSSLERR_NO_CONTEXT_YET

entry.title: Re: Customer wants to perform custom pro-ration in Variab...
entry.content:
Hi @Bhavyapurohit1 

Ideally you don't want to put it as assignment level but at summary level as a final trim to payout.

It is possible to do it at assignment level, I've done it before for a big customer in retail, but then you need to completely customize variable pay. When you fully customize variable pay and don't use the standard logic for proration anymore, there is almost no logic you can't build.

I don't have an example handy I could share as it was all customer configuration which we cannot share, all I can say is that it takes a very seasoned consultant on VarPay to build assignment level impact on proration logic through ALCF.

Best,

Xavier

entry.title: Re: SAP Document AI Workspace: Features You Need to Know
entry.content:
@pvsbprasad 

SAP Document AI Workspace is basically the control room for document processing. It’s where you set up document types, decide how incoming files should be handled, and keep an eye on the whole process.

In simple words, you can:

  • define what kind of document you’re dealing with,

  • set up the flow for processing it,

  • review anything that needs human checking,

  • and track how everything is performing.

So instead of manually handling invoices, purchase orders, or other business documents, the workspace helps automate that work and keeps the messy exceptions under control.

entry.title: Re: 4-Eyes by Configuration: Multi-Processor Workflow Approvals in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
@SBach 

This blog is about setting up multi-processor workflow approvals in SAP S/4HANA so you can enforce a true 4-eyes or even 6-eyes principle.

In simple words:

  • One person starts the process.

  • Another person, or even two others, must approve it.

  • The same user cannot both create and approve the request.

  • This is done by configuration, not custom code.

Why it matters:

  • It adds stronger control for sensitive approvals.

  • It helps prevent self-approval and reduces approval abuse.

  • It’s useful for finance, master data, and other governed processes.

entry.title: Re: iOS Simulator crashes with reference error in sap ser...
entry.content:
Dear Pragyan and team,
Per our research:  The error:  "  ReferenceError: UIContentSizeCategorySmall is not defined"
is a known issue occurring in certain versions of the Mobile Development Kit (MDK) client when running on the iOS Simulator. It is typically triggered by a mismatch in the NativeScript accessibility modules or an issue with how the simulator handles Dynamic Type scaling.

Typical steps to do:

1) Ensure that the MDK Plugin SDK version you are using matches the SSAM 2405 metadata requirements. As documented in SAP Note 2933065, always check the matrix to see if there are specific patches or "TCI corrections" required for your build. If you have not recently updated your SDK plugins, perform a clean install of the dependencies in your MDKClient_SDK directory.

2) Clean the Project Environment:
Simulators often cache old build artifacts, including conflicting accessibility or UI resources. Before rebuilding, perform a full cleanup:

  • Delete the platforms/ios folder in your client project.
  • Clear the node_modules directory and package-lock.json.
  • Wipe the iOS Simulator data (Simulator > Device > Erase All Content and Settings).
  • Run npm install again to fetch clean dependencies, then rebuild the client.

3) Check Xcode or simulator: The UIContentSizeCategorySmall error is frequently related to iOS "Dynamic Type" (Accessibility) settings.

If you are testing on the simulator, ensure your Xcode and simulator versions are aligned with the supported versions listed in the MDK Dependency Matrix.

Some developers have resolved this by ensuring the iOS deployment target in Xcode is set correctly (e.g., matching the minimum version supported by the current SSAM MDK client) and by resetting the simulator's accessibility font scale to "Default" if it was modified.
This specific error has been reported in the SAP Community forums for SSAM 2405 implementations. If the above steps do not resolve the crash, please check the specific thread on the SAP Community (linked in your references) for any recently released patches or workarounds posted by the SAP MDK engineering team, as they may have issued a specific hotfix for the accessibility module.

Best Practice:  Since this issue is simulator-specific, it is highly recommended to verify the build on a physical device. If the branded client launches successfully on a real iPhone/iPad, the issue is confirmed to be an environment-specific conflict within the simulator's runtime rather than a flaw in your metadata or configuration.

This is what we can share at the moment.  Hopefully other members in our community may have additional expertise or experience that they can share.

Best to you.

entry.title: Re: ODATA - FML_UPLMATINVPR_SRV usage
entry.content:
I did some research and here is a checklist you may try.

The idea here is to leverage some logs to see and pinpoint where the issue is.  So the checklist below may help your team check the valid/needed logs to pre-determine what may be causing the issue.

1. Payload & Header Verification
[ ] CSRF Token: Ensure the code first performs a GET request to retrieve the x-csrf-token and passes it back in the POST header.

[ ] Slug Header: Verify if the service requires a Slug header (common in SAP upload services to define the filename).

[ ] Content-Type: Ensure the header is set precisely to multipart/form-data with the correct boundary string if required by the service definition.

2. Environment & Authority Check
[ ] Service Authentication: Does the user context executing the ABAP code have authorization for both the OData service and the underlying business logic (e.g., M_MATE_WRK)?

[ ] MIME/App Server Access: Confirm that the technical user executing the code has read access to the specific path on the Application Server (transaction AL11) where the Excel file resides.

3. Execution Simulation
[ ] Trace Comparison: Try to run a Fiori upload while the trace is active in /IWFND/TRACES. Compare the HTTP request headers generated by the Fiori framework with the headers their ABAP code is sending.

Note (Additional Idea):  Instead of CL_HTTP_CLIENT (which is legacy), try to use the CL_WEB_HTTP_CLIENT or the CL_HTTP_DESTINATION_PROVIDER frameworks if you are on an S/4HANA 2023 environment. These offer better integration with the current SAP connectivity.

These are just some idea to hopefully expose the issue.  Then the experts from the team can act on it.

entry.title: Re: Saving Variants and Layouts after upgrade
entry.content:
Dear team,
Per my experience on post upgrade of any SAP S/4HANA to a much newer system always is tied to certain features that got deprecated and newer functionalities that impacts POST upgrade experience.

Think of it this way, older system uses older UI or code libraries, when migrating to a newer system, if older code cannot be used in the newer system then any saved variants or parameters will be dismissed and disappear.
So to answer this question, I just know that certain things may occur and I will simply share all the fixes/notes/expectation or limitation I can find on older upgrades and hopefully you can consume any of them to get some insights.  This is not necessarily tied to the version of S/4HANA 2025 but it is more that we expect deprecation to occur so stuff may disappear.

Some known References:

1) 2726048 - Personal saved filters missing after the upgrade - SAP for Me
2) 3343688 - Variants Created Before S/4Hana 2021 Upgrade Cannot Be Deleted - SAP for Me
3) 3196205 - How to migrate variants/views of UI5 apps from one system to another - SAP for Me
4) 2789848 - Migration report for homepage and SAPUI5 application personalization - SAP for Me
5) 2848194 - VA05 - Display Variant Missing after Upgrade from ECC to S/4HANA - SAP for Me
6) 3439338 - How to find variants saved in fiori apps - SAP for Me
7) 2236210 - Web Intelligence reports that contain variants have the variants removed after the migration - SAP for Me

Again the goal is to show that certain variants may change after a post upgrade and try to consume some of the information above to simply set your expectation.

Like anything new, logs and tracking and documentation is needed to really capture what occurs.

Hopefully this information assist.

entry.title: Stop Users from Typing Invalid Values in SAPUI5 ComboBox – A Practical Validation Approach
entry.content:
Introduction

In one of my recent SAPUI5 projects, I needed users to select a value from a predefined list. Since the list was quite large, I wanted to make the selection process as smooth as possible. Instead of using sap.m.Select, I went with sap.m.ComboBox because it lets users search for values by typing, making it much easier to find the right option.

Everything worked as expected until I started writing unit test cases. While testing different scenarios, I noticed that users weren't limited to selecting values from the dropdown they could also type any value they wanted. Even if the entered text didn't exist in the list, the ComboBox would still accept it.

It might seem like obvious behavior once you know how ComboBox works, but it was something I hadn't considered during development. That's when I realized that while the control offers a great user experience, it doesn't automatically guarantee valid input.

We could have used a value help dialog for selection, but the requirement specifically called for a dropdown-based experience. That’s why the focus stayed on ComboBox  and a deeper validation approach around it.

In this article, I'll walk through the approach I used to keep the search functionality intact while ensuring users can only submit values that actually exist in the dropdown.

 

The Real Issue in Applications

In actual business scenarios, this becomes a real problem when data moves to the backend. Users may enter free text in the ComboBox and it still gets treated as valid input even if it doesn’t exist in the master data.

This can lead to invalid payloads, failed backend validations, and inconsistent business data in reports. What starts as a small UI behavior can quickly turn into a data integrity issue.

That’s why, when using sap.m.ComboBox for strictly controlled fields like Country, Plant, or Company Code, frontend validation becomes essential before processing the input.

 Picture 1: Understanding the ComboBox and It's Behaviour

 

The Approach I Used

To handle this, I added a simple validation on the ComboBox events. The idea was straightforward—accept the input only if it matches a valid item from the list.

I mainly relied on the change event along with getSelectedKey(). If the user entered something that didn’t match any item, I cleared the value, showed an error state using setValueState("Error"), and displayed a meaningful message.

I also used selectionChange to reset the error state when a valid option was selected from the dropdown, ensuring a smoother user experience.

 

Implementation

The validation was handled mainly using the change and selectionChange events. The goal was to ensure that only valid values from the dropdown are accepted, while giving clear feedback when something incorrect is entered.

XML View Code: (.xml file)
<!-- XML View -->
<ComboBox
    id="countryCombo"
    items="{path: '/Countries'}"
    selectionChange=".onSelectionChange"
    change=".onChange">
    <core:Item
        key="{CountryCode}"
        text="{CountryName}" />
</ComboBox>
Controller Code: (.js file)
// Controller Logic
onChange: function (oEvent) {
    const oCombo = oEvent.getSource();
    const sValue = oCombo.getValue().trim();
    const sKey = oCombo.getSelectedKey();
    if (!sValue || !sKey) {
        oCombo.setValue("");
        oCombo.setValueState("Error");
        oCombo.setValueStateText("Please select a valid value from the list.");
        return;
    }
    oCombo.setValueState("None");
},

onSelectionChange: function (oEvent) {
    oEvent.getSource().setValueState("None");
}

 

Best Practices

When working with sap.m.ComboBox, validation should always be treated as part of the design, not an afterthought.

Use getSelectedKey() instead of relying only on getValue(), since free text input is always possible. Trim user input before validation to avoid whitespace-related issues.

Always provide clear ValueState feedback so users immediately understand what went wrong. Also, reset the error state on valid selection using selectionChange to improve usability.

Finally, even if frontend validation is in place, always enforce the same checks at the backend to ensure data integrity.

 Picture 2: Data Integrity

 

Alternative Approaches

Depending on the requirement, there are a few other ways to handle this.

If search is not critical and the list is small, sap.m.Select is a simpler option since it only allows predefined selections.

In some cases, developers try disabling free typing in ComboBox, but that removes the search flexibility, which is often the main reason for choosing it in the first place.

Another option is model or backend-level validation, but that should always complement frontend checks rather than replace them.

  Picture 3: Right Approaches for Handling Selections

 

Key Takeaway & Conclusion:

A ComboBox is not automatically restricted to its items. If your requirement is to allow only predefined values, you must explicitly validate the user input before processing it.

The goal is not to remove the flexibility of sap.m.ComboBox, but to balance it—keep the search experience intact while ensuring only valid, business-approved values make it to the backend.

Kindly Note: We could have used a value help dialog for selection, but the requirement specifically called for a dropdown-based experience. That’s why the focus stayed on sap.m.ComboBox and a deeper validation approach around it.

If you have any alternative effective solutions, please feel free to share your thoughts and so everyone can learn from each other.

Thank you for taking the time to read this blog! If you found this helpful, i would love to hear your thoughts, feedback or questions in the comments. Let's keep learning and growing together!

I’m still new to blogging, so if you notice anything that could be improved or corrected, please don’t hesitate to let me know!!

entry.title: Managing Datasphere Consumption: Gaining Visibility and Control Over Capacity Units
entry.content:
The Challenge

As organizations increasingly adopt SAP Datasphere to power data integration, modeling, and analytics workloads, managing consumption has become a critical aspect of operational success. However, one of the most common challenges customers face today is a lack of timely visibility into their Datasphere Capacity Unit (CU) usage.

In many cases, consumption is driven by multiple services running in parallel—such as Premium Outbound Integration, data replication tasks, or scheduled transformations. These services can scale dynamically based on workload demands, which is great for performance but less predictable from a cost perspective.

The issue? Customers often only discover they have exceeded their allocated or expected CU consumption after receiving their monthly bill. By that time, it is too late to take corrective action, and unexpected costs can impact both IT budgets and business planning cycles.

For organizations operating with fixed or forecasted budgets, this lack of real-time insight introduces risk:

  • Difficulty aligning IT consumption with financial planning
  • Limited ability to react to unexpected workload spikes
  • Reduced confidence in scaling Datasphere usage across the business
 

Key Insight

Cost transparency in cloud environments is not just about tracking individual services—it’s about understanding aggregate consumption in context of your budget.

While it is helpful to know how much each service consumes, what truly matters to decision-makers is:

  • How total consumption compares to purchased capacity
  • Whether current usage trends will exceed budget thresholds
  • Which services or workloads are driving unexpected increases

In other words, organizations need a holistic view of consumption, coupled with proactive alerts that signal when they are approaching limits—not just retrospective reports after costs have already been incurred.

 

The Solution: Enhanced FinOps Capabilities in SAP BTP

SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) addresses this challenge through an enhanced FinOps (Financial Operations) dashboard, available in the Costs and Usage section of the BTP Cockpit.

This dashboard provides:

  • Centralized visibility into Datasphere capacity unit consumption
  • Detailed breakdowns by service, environment, and time period
  • Trend analysis to identify usage patterns over time
  • Budget monitoring tools to align consumption with financial targets

Most importantly, it enables organizations to move from a reactive cost management approach to a proactive FinOps strategy.

A key feature is the ability to configure threshold-based alerts. These alerts notify administrators and stakeholders when consumption approaches defined limits—such as 70%, 85%, or 100% of allocated capacity.

Here is a quick demo:

1) Open BTP Cockpit and select Cost and Usage

 

2) From the usage option, you can see the usage of Datasphere components.

 

3) In Datasphere,  the System > Configuration > Tenant Configuration should show similar consumption. 

4) Our engineering teams will also be making improvements into the Monitoring > Capacities page to contain historical monitoring. This should be available very soon ** details in appendix **

 

 5) You can utilize the Budget area of the dashboard to alert Administrators when the budget reaches a threshold.

 

 

The Outcome

By leveraging the enhanced FinOps dashboard and setting consumption thresholds, organizations can avoid unexpected costs, improve budget accuracy, optimize resource utilization and enable better decision-making. 

 

Appendix

Our engineering teams are making improvements to the Monitor > Capacities page. Our visions is that this page will be the central area to view consumption in Datasphere. Here are some improvements we are making:

1) Spaces - show consumption based on spaces. So that you can get clarity into which Spaces are using the resources

 

2) Granularity - Increase granularity of what makes up a Datasphere capacity unit.

Before:

 

Mock-up on Improvements (In development and subject to change):

entry.title: Re: BTE 1820 custom code is not triggering during payment...
entry.content: If BTE 1820 is shown as active but your breakpoint is never reached, the event is likely not being called in your specific payment scenario. Double-check that the correct product is assigned in FIBF, the function module is active, and that the payment method or document type actually triggers BTE 1820. An external breakpoint in the function module can also help confirm whether the event is being executed at all.

entry.title: KSII - Maessage KD301
entry.content:
Currently, the client confirms activities in seconds, however the activity types master data are as hours. Regarding the conversion of entries for cost valuation is being converted and valued correctly.

The issue is when we are using the actual activities quantities like base to allocate costs cycles and actual cost rate, the SAP don’t find the activities quantities by hours confirmed to allocation determination.

Cost center CTR 1000/1001100001, cost element 41202001: Tracing factor for splitting is zero

Message No. KD301

Diagnosis

The plan/actual costs for cost element 41202001 cannot be settled to the CO activities for period 5 according to assignment rule MOD in cost center CTR 1000/1001100001 because the tracing factor in plan and in actual is zero.

System Response

The plan/actual costs for cost element 41202001 are split according to the equivalence numbers for the activity types.

Procedure

Check assignment MOD and your splitting rule.

entry.title: Make ChatGPT or Claude generate correct ABAP syntax - a free, 2-minute setup
entry.content:
AI assistants write ABAP that looks right and then fails the syntax check — obsolete statements, RAP behavior definitions that don't parse, classic-only syntax in supposedly Cloud-ready code. The fix isn't a better prompt. It's giving the assistant what every other language already gives it: a syntax checker it can call while it works, so errors get caught and fixed before the code ever reaches you.

I built that missing piece after 15 years of ABAP and open-sourced it (MIT): abap-mcp, which runs abaplint (Lars Hvam's open-source ABAP parser, ~180 rules) as a tool your AI assistant can call — entirely offline, no SAP system, no credentials. Here's the full setup.

Step 1 — Prerequisite. Node.js 20+ on your machine (nodejs.org). That's it.

Step 2 — Connect your assistant (Claude Code, Cursor, VS Code agent mode, or any MCP client). One command:

claude mcp add abap-mcp -- npx -y abap-mcp

claude mcp add abap-mcp -- npx -y abap-mcp

(For Cursor/VS Code: add { "mcpServers": { "abap-mcp": { "command": "npx", "args": ["-y", "abap-mcp"] } } } to your MCP config.)

Step 3 — Make the checking automatic. Add one line to your project notes (CLAUDE.md / Cursor rules):

Before claiming any ABAP change is done, lint it with abap-mcp. For ABAP Cloud targets, run the readiness check too.

From now on the assistant writes ABAP → lints it → gets exact parser errors with line numbers → fixes → re-lints — and only shows you code that parses clean. You'll see it happen in the tool-call log.

Step 4 — Using plain ChatGPT (no MCP)? Use the same engine as a command line and check the generated code yourself before it goes near a transport:

npx abap-mcp lint zcl_generated.clas.abap
npx abap-mcp readiness src/ --fail-below 80    # whole abapGit repo: ABAP Cloud readiness score

Step 5 — Try these prompts once connected:

"Lint this class against ABAP Cloud and fix every finding."

"Is this report cloud-ready? Categorize the blockers."

"Scaffold a draft-enabled RAP BO for entity Booking, table zbooking, key booking_id." (every generated class and CDS view is validated through the parser before you see it)

"Explain why exit_or_check was flagged, with the Clean ABAP rationale."

What it honestly can and can't catch: everything the parser sees — syntax, statement validity per ABAP language version (including Cloud), structure, Clean ABAP style. It does not know your DDIC (a wrong field name on a table the assistant wasn't shown still gets through — provide the dependent objects in the same call to close that), and released-API compliance remains ATC's job; the tool says so on every report. It complements ADT-connected MCP servers (including SAP's upcoming one) — they're the hands on a live system, this is the feedback loop where your files live.

Repo with a cookbook (PR review without a transport, migration triage, CI gates, agent workflows): github.com/palimkarakshay/abap-mcp · npm: abap-mcp

I'd really value your feedback — try it on your own Z-code and tell me: What does your assistant still get wrong (syntax, obsolete statements, BDEF structure, Cloud restrictions)? Did the readiness categories match how you'd size remediation? What would you want checked offline that today forces a system round-trip? Replies here or GitHub issues both welcome — that's what the next version gets built around.

(Not affiliated with SAP; SAP, ABAP and RAP are SAP SE trademarks.)

entry.title: SAP Cloud ALM Operations Troubleshooting for S/4HANA: Real Project Challenges and Lessons Learned
entry.content:
SAP Cloud ALM plays a critical role in enabling end-to-end operations monitoring for S/4HANA systems, covering multiple areas such as system health, job monitoring, integration monitoring, exception tracking, and more. While these capabilities provide strong visibility into system behavior, implementing and operating them in real project scenarios often comes with practical challenges.

During our S/4HANA project, we encountered several issues across different monitoring areas in SAP Cloud ALM Operations—ranging from configuration gaps and data inconsistencies to unexpected behavior in monitoring applications. Many of these challenges were not straightforward and required detailed analysis and troubleshooting to identify the root cause.

In this blog, we share our real project experience by highlighting the key issues we faced, along with their root causes, solutions, and workarounds. The aim is to provide a practical troubleshooting guide that can help others quickly identify and resolve similar challenges in SAP Cloud ALM Operations.


1. Health monitoring use case not getting activated:

During the setup of SAP Cloud ALM Operations, we encountered an issue where the Health Monitoring use case could not be activated. Even after enabling the health monitoring use case check, the configuration did not remain stable and was getting automatically unchecked, preventing successful activation:


If you are facing a similar issue, follow the below steps to fix it:
Go to the CALM tenant and click on Operations->Health Monitoring:

select a scope (system that you would want to configure for Health monitoring):

 

click on Apply:

Goto configuration->Managed components->Systems and switch on the data collection for the system in your scope:

 

Now, come back to the s/4 system in which you were activating the use case and go to the tcode   /SDF/ALM_SETUP, click on the activate use case button and check the use case for "Health monitoring" again and click on continue:


Now, click on "Refresh" button and you will see the below message:

Health Monitoring is now activated.

2. Runtime status showing errors across all monitoring after use case activation:

Although the configuration status was active and all setups were completed successfully, the runtime status across monitoring still showed errors, leading to confusion during validation.

There can be many reasons why the runtime status goes into error, to troubleshoot it you can:
1. switch off the data collection for 10 minutes for all the use cases and switch it on again:

If the above solution does not fix your error as it did not fix ours, you can:
2. Re-register the system: Unregister the system by using tcode:/SDF/ALM_SETUP->Unregister button and re-register it back by clicking on Register button:

 check the runtime status of the monitoring use cases, if it is successful, great, if not you can try the below fix that resolved the issue in our project:
3.  Check the CALM heartbeat job in the s/4 system ->SM37 ->job name as *heartbeat*:
check if the job is getting canceled, if yes, check the job logs, ours was an authorization error:


"No authorization for Destination XXXX"
check su53 for the user used to schedule the heartbeat job and resolve the authorization issue by providing the roles missing to the user:

The runtime status should be successful now.

3. Job Monitoring dashboard showed "No Data" despite active configuration and setup:

After configuration and use case activation, the runtime status initially showed success, but no job data appeared in the dashboard; after some time, the runtime status also turned to "Error":

1. switch off the data collection for 10 minutes for all the use cases and switch it on again:

If the above solution does not fix your error, you can:

2. Re-register the system: Unregister the system by using tcode:/SDF/ALM_SETUP->Unregister button and re-register it back by clicking on Register button:

 

If the issue persists, you can try the approach that worked for us: verify if Job Monitoring is activated in client 000. If yes, deactivate it in client 000, re-register the system, and then activate the use case in the intended client for monitoring.

 

These scenarios demonstrate that even with technically correct configuration and successful use case activation, discrepancies can still arise between configuration status and runtime behavior in SAP Cloud ALM Operations. Issues such as client-level inconsistencies, data collection gaps, or system registration misalignment can impact monitoring outcomes and require deeper validation.

If you have faced any issues in Operations, feel free to share them in the comments. Also, let us know how you resolved them—your inputs can greatly help others in the community.

entry.title: Re: System based assessment issue on Submitting SAP Certification C_CPE_2601 SAP CAP Backend Develop

I have followed word by word instructions and in fact got a fully working solution and all the results 100% traceable as asked by SAP. I had raised about 5 cases with SAP and they kept re-crediting my attempts but I see no use of it if their AI assessment solution cannot be fixed to evaluate the exam correctly. They also mentioned about using the assigned group number to replace all the ### while creating all the elements in the assessment. However the assessment document did not have any group number and in fact asked me to assign the user id against my account inside the practice environment to be used to replace all the ##, which I did.

entry.title: Re: Entering the New Era of Agentic AI for ABAP Development
entry.content:
Hi Community,

"

Custom Code Agents for SAP S/4HANA Migration 

SAP S/4HANA migration projects carry significant complexity, and a large part of that complexity lies in custom ABAP code — business logic built up over many years, often undocumented and tightly coupled with SAP standard objects. Understanding it, adapting it, and validating it has historically been time consuming and expensive work.  

The Custom Code Assistant addresses this directly, bringing together a coherent, intelligent toolchain that covers all phases of an SAP S/4HANA custom code migration project. Three agents focusing on developer execution will be delivered by ABAP platform, complemented by additional agents from SAP Cloud ALM handling broader transformation governance. 

"

 

Thanks for your sharing, it really help us a lot.

 

🙂

Regards,

ZFiori.

entry.title: Re: Flexible Workflow – Multiple Workflow Determination f...
entry.content:
@akash_r18  I’ve done similar. You’re going to need to implement the below two BADIs.

  • SWF_WORKFLOW_CONDITION_DEF
  • SWF_WORKFLOW_CONDITION_EVAL

the first BADI makes a value appear as a start condition, and the second assesses it.  You’d likely need to provide your ABAPER the CDS views that are released for key user development, as this will originate in the 100 tenant of DEV.  For example I_PRODUCT could be used for  material group, or if you allow manual overrides, both pieces of info would come from released CDS I_PurchaseOrderAPI01

entry.content:
If you want to become a Cloud Admin, the process and prerequisites are documented in the following resources:
- KBA 2596214 - How to maintain Super, Cloud, and User Administrator S-user IDs listed in My Important Contacts - SAP for Me
- KBA 2492132 and the online help documentation for the Administrator Concept

For your convenience, I have captured screenshots of the Cloud Admin explanations from these materials for reference.

Based on my investigation, your S-user ID S0025823361 is currently missing two mandatory permissions needed to become a Cloud Admin: Display Cloud Data and Edit Cloud Data. Because these permissions are not present, your S-user is not a Cloud Admin at the moment.

Please kindly note that SAP applies strict user management policies. I am not authorized to assign the Cloud Admin role to your S-user if your company already has an active Cloud Admin. This safeguard is designed to protect your company’s data security.

Next steps:

please contact your company’s Cloud Admin and ask them to assign the Cloud Admin role to you. They can follow the step-by-step guidance in the KBAs below. 
Cloud: Super Administrator

1511008 - How to add or change S-user ID authorizations - SAP for Me

1614304 - How to use the Copy Authorization feature for S-user IDs - SAP for Me

entry.title: Re: Manage Customer Line Items – Net Days and WBS Element...
entry.content:
Hi Pablo,

Thank you for raising this question. I would like to share the following findings that may help clarify the behavior you observed.

1. Days Net Field

The Days Net field corresponds to the field BSEG-ZBD3T, which is derived from the payment terms configuration in SSCUI 102934 - Maintain Terms of Payment. Based on my testing in an internal system, the field is populated correctly once the relevant settings are properly maintained in SSCUI 102934. I would recommend verifying your payment terms configuration there. See KBA  3774079 - Days Net field displays 0 in Manage Customer Line Items app - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me for more details.

2. WBS Element Field

The Manage Customer Line Items app displays line items from a financial operations perspective, showing invoiced amounts, due dates, and dunning status. Information related to WBS elements, profit centers, and segments belongs to G/L Accounting and is not displayed in this app by design. To view WBS element information, please use the Display Line Items in General Ledger app instead. For further details, please refer to KBA 3346815 - Profit Center not displayed in Manage Supplier Line Items and Manage Customer Line Items app - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me and Manage Customer Line Items: FAQ.

I hope the above information is helpful. Feel free to reach out if you have any further questions.

Best regards,
Gordon

entry.title: Unable to delete S-User ID from the User List in SAP for Me
entry.content:
Dear SAP Support, 

We attempted to delete the S User ID SPHONE_NUMBER (Arimurti Pramuditta), but the system blocks the deletion with the following error. 
"BP Relationship DIGITS and DIGITS has active transaction and cannot be deleted"

Please help me to delete the S User SPHONE_NUMBER as he already resigned from the company.

Thank you so much. 

-PERSON_NAME-


--- Steps to Reproduce ---

  1. Login in SAP for Me
  2. Dashboards > Users & Contacts > Partner Contacts > Manage Users > All Users
  3. Find SPHONE_NUMBER > Click Delete

entry.title: Re: Unable to delete S-User ID from the User List in SAP...
entry.content:
I understand you have an issue that could not delete S-user*.

I reviewed our backend and confirmed that S-user * cannot be deleted because it is assigned as a Partner Contact. For background and guidance on Partner Contacts, please see KBA 1271517 - Important Contact descriptions and how to view/change S-users with important functions - SAP for Me. 

However the authorizations required for Partner Dashboards are different than Customer Dashboards. We here at SAP Support do not have relevant authorization to support SAP for Me Partner Dashboards or any of the related partner cards.

To resolve your access problem, please refer to the steps and attached screenshots below:

  • Navigate to the designated URL： https://partneredge.sap.com/en/welcome.html
  • Click Get Support to reach out to the dedicated Partner Support team for assistance.

Additionally, I would like to clarify the process handling for this case. This is an SAE case rather than a standard support case. You have booked a 30-minute SAE session as below, and per internal process, I am required to join the session to discuss the issue with you during the meeting.

Appointment has been scheduled on *.

Subject: Unable to delete S-User ID from the User List in SAP for Me

However, our team does not possess the authorization to resolve partner-related inquiries. If I attend this scheduled session, it will only delay the resolution of your issue.

For this reason, I have shared the correct resolution steps with you ahead of the session start time.

After reviewing the guidance, you may follow the proper procedure to reach out to the Partner Support team for dedicated assistance.
